---
title: "Latent Variables Moderated Mediation Using Correct and Misspecified Models"
shorttitle: "Moderated Mediation With Latent Variables"

author:
  - name: Felipe Fontana Vieira
    corresponding: true
    orcid: 0009-0006-0949-6569
    email: felipe.fontanavieira@ugent.be
    affiliations:
      - name: Ghent University
        department: Department of Data Analysis, Faculty of Psychology and Educational Sciences
        address: Henri Dunantlaan 2
        city: Ghent
        country: Belgium
        postal-code: 9000
        id: UGent
    roles:
      - Conceptualization
      - Methodology
      - Software
      - Investigation
      - Data curation
      - Formal analysis
      - Visualization
      - Writing - original draft
      - Writing - review & editing
  - name: Yves Rosseel
    corresponding: false
    orcid: 0000-0002-4129-4477
    affiliations:
      - ref: UGent
    roles:
      - Conceptualization
      - Methodology
      - Software
      - Supervision
      - Writing - review & editing
      - Funding acquisition

author-note:
  status-changes:
    affiliation-change: ~
    deceased: ~
  disclosures:
    study-registration: "This study was preregistered."
    data-sharing: "The preregistration, data, code, and materials for this 
    manuscript can be found at <https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20703258>."
    financial-support: "This project was funded by the Special Research Fund 
    (BOF) of Ghent University (BOF/24J/2023/144)."

abstract: |
  TODO: Abstract.

impact-statement: |
  TODO: Translational abstract.

keywords: [moderated mediation, structural equation modeling, latent interaction, measurement error, model misspecification]

language:
  title-impact-statement: "Translational Abstract"

word-count: true
floatsintext: false
numbered-lines: false
link-citations: false
mask: false

bibliography: "references.bib"

format:
  apaquarto-pdf:
    documentmode: man
    keep-tex: false
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \raggedbottom
        \usepackage{pdflscape}

execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

Mediation analysis is a well-known procedure in the behavioral and social
sciences [@Hayes2018; @Preacher2007; @Pieters2017; @Charlton2021]. In its 
basic and standard form, the effect of an independent variable on an outcome is 
decomposed into a direct effect and an indirect effect transmitted through a 
mediator, with all variables observed. This basic single mediator model has 
since been extended, for example by allowing the indirect effect to depend on 
a moderator and by adjusting for covariates [@Hayes2015; @Edwards2007; 
@Preacher2007; @Montoya2024].

A more fundamental concern arises from measurement. Many constructs in 
psychology are not directly observed but are measured indirectly through 
multiple items, that is, they are latent variables [@BollenHoyle2012].
When such constructs are reduced to summary scores 
(e.g., a sum or mean of the items) and analyzed as though they were observed, 
measurement error is ignored. This error can bias the indirect effect and 
inflate its uncertainty [@HoyleKenny1999; @ColePreacher2014], and it is 
especially problematic for the mediator, which acts at once as a predictor and 
an outcome [@GonzalezMacKinnon2021]. To address this, structural equation 
modelling (SEM) with latent variables has been proposed, especially in 
the context of mediation [@CheungLau2008;
@FalkBiesanz2015; @LedgerwoodShrout2011; @Montoya2024]. 
By estimating structural relationships between common factors, from 
which indicator-specific unique variance has been partitioned, provided the 
measurement model is correctly specified [@Bollen1989; @Rhemtulla2020]. 
The problem is compounded under moderation, where the product term is even 
less reliable than either of its constituents [@Bohrnstedt1978; @Busemeyer1983]; 
yet we also note that latent variables do not necessarily improve power 
in general, whether for interactions or not [@Cham2012; @Fuller1987; 
@LedgerwoodShrout2011; @WangRhemtulla2021].

Moderation with latent variables is not straightforward in terms of
estimation. Indeed, a latent interaction involves the product of two latent 
variables, which is unobserved and nonnormal even when its constituents are 
normal [@KleinMoosbrugger2000]. A number of methods have been proposed to
estimate latent interactions [e.g., @AlginaMoulder2001; @Arminger1998;
@AsparouhovMuthen2021; @Boker2023; @Bollen1995; @CoxKelcey2021; @HolstBudtz2020;
@JoreskogYan1996; @KennyJudd1984; @KleinMoosbrugger2000; @KleinMuthen2007;
@Marsh2004; @MooijaartBentler2010; @MooijaartSatorra2012; @WallAmemiya2000]. 
Most adopt a joint ("system-wide") estimation
strategy, in which the measurement and structural parameters are estimated
simultaneously. This is the case of the most known methods: latent moderated
structural equations (LMS) and product-indicator (PI). Opposed to these 
are structural-after-measurement (SAM) approaches [@CoxKelcey2021; 
@HolstBudtz2020; @NgChan2020; @RosseelLoh2024; @WallAmemiya2001; @WallAmemiya2003],
in which the measurement model is estimated first, from the indicators alone, 
and the structural model is estimated afterward with the measurement parameters 
held fixed. Most of the abovementioned approaches have so far been rarely applied in 
practice, most likely held back by limited software, slow estimation, and 
error-prone implementations. With recent open-source implementations 
[@HolstBudtz2020; @Rosseel2025; @Slupphaug2025] and non-commercial software 
[@Keller2025], this is likely to change.

In this context, a further consideration concerns interpretation. Mediation is 
a hypothesis about causal effects, and causal interpretation requires 
identification assumptions that *may* not be verified from the data alone 
[@Imai2010; @Morelli2026; @Rohrer2022; @VanderWeele2015]. With latent variable 
models, further caution is needed [@Sterner2024; @VanderWeeleVansteelandt2022]. 
We focus on one that is central to interpretation: the estimated latent 
variables must validly represent the intended constructs 
[@GonzalezMacKinnon2021]. In fact, when they do not, the model is subject to 
interpretational confounding [@Burt1976], a discrepancy between the empirical 
meaning assigned to a latent variable through its estimated 
measurement parameters and the meaning intended by the researcher. Under 
system-wide estimation, the measurement and structural parameters are fit
jointly, so the estimated loadings, and with them the empirical meaning of a
latent variable, can change with the structural model that is imposed. The 
latent variable is then no longer anchored to its indicators alone, and 
inferences about it need not be consistent across structural models. SAM avoids
this by estimating the measurement model on its own. Thus, because the structural 
model cannot then influence it, the estimated latent variables, and their 
meaning, do not depend on the structure subsequently fit, and interpretational 
confounding is avoided. Note, however, that this does not imply the 
structure is correct [@VanderWeeleVansteelandt2022]. Local SAM (LSAM) is 
one such method, which was recently extended to interaction and quadratic effects 
among latent variables [@Rosseel2025], which makes it applicable to 
latent variable moderated mediation.

To illustrate it, we take a mediation model with three latent variables, X, M, and
Y, each with three indicators, and obtain a population covariance matrix from a
correctly specified version. We then alter it so that the covariances between 
the X and M indicators are unequal across the X indicators (3.0, 1.5, and 1.5, 
with mean held at the implied value of 2.0), changing only their pattern, 
not the overall X-M strength. To the same data we fit two structural models, 
one omitting the mediator and one including it, by system-wide SEM and by SAM 
($N = 200$), and compare the estimated loadings of X. As shown in 
@tbl-interp-confounding, the loadings of X estimated by system-wide 
SEM depend on the structural model: once the mediator enters, the loadings of 
$x_2$ and $x_3$ fall below their true value of one, even though the measurement 
of X is unchanged, so the empirical meaning of X shifts with the structure 
imposed. SAM estimates the measurement model from the indicators alone, so 
the X loadings are identical with and without the mediator and stay at their
true values.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-interp-confounding
#| tbl-cap: "Illustration for Interpretational Confounding."
library(lavaan)

model.pop <- '
  X =~ 1*x1 + 1*x2 + 1*x3
  Y =~ 1*y1 + 1*y2 + 1*y3
  M =~ 1*m1 + 1*m2 + 1*m3
  X ~~ 4*X
  M ~~ 3.0*M
  Y ~~ 2.76*Y
  x1 ~~ 1.7*x1; x2 ~~ 1.7*x2; x3 ~~ 1.7*x3
  y1 ~~ 1.7*y1; y2 ~~ 1.7*y2; y3 ~~ 1.7*y3
  m1 ~~ 1.7*m1; m2 ~~ 1.7*m2; m3 ~~ 1.7*m3
  M ~ 0.5*X
  Y ~ 0.1*X + 0.5*M
'
Sigma <- lavInspect(sem(model.pop), "implied")$cov

Sigma2 <- Sigma
Sigma2[c("m1", "m2", "m3"), "x1"] <- 3.0
Sigma2[c("m1", "m2", "m3"), "x2"] <- 1.5
Sigma2[c("m1", "m2", "m3"), "x3"] <- 1.5
Sigma2["x1", c("m1", "m2", "m3")] <- 3.0
Sigma2["x2", c("m1", "m2", "m3")] <- 1.5
Sigma2["x3", c("m1", "m2", "m3")] <- 1.5

model_nomed <- '
  X =~ x1 + x2 + x3
  Y =~ y1 + y2 + y3
  Y ~ X
'
model_med <- '
  X =~ x1 + x2 + x3
  Y =~ y1 + y2 + y3
  M =~ m1 + m2 + m3
  M ~ X
  Y ~ M + X
'
fit_it <- function(estimator, model) {
  estimator(model, sample.cov = Sigma2, sample.cov.rescale = FALSE,
            sample.nobs = 200L)
}
xload <- function(fit) lavInspect(fit, "est")$lambda[c("x1", "x2", "x3"), "X"]

tab <- data.frame(
  Parameter = c("Loading of $x_1$", "Loading of $x_2$", "Loading of $x_3$"),
  True = c(1, 1, 1),
  `SEM, no M` = xload(fit_it(sem, model_nomed)),
  `SEM, with M` = xload(fit_it(sem, model_med)),
  `SAM, no M` = xload(fit_it(sam, model_nomed)),
  `SAM, with M` = xload(fit_it(sam, model_med)),
  check.names = FALSE
)
knitr::asis_output(paste0("\\begin{center}\n",
  knitr::kable(tab, digits = 2, booktabs = TRUE, escape = FALSE, 
               format = "latex"),
  "\n\\end{center}"))
```

The example involves a single, deliberate change to the structural model,
but it reflects a general property of system-wide estimation. That is, because the
measurement and structural parameters are estimated jointly, the consequences of
a misspecification are not confined to the part of the model in which it occurs
[@BainterBollen2014]. Prior simulation work has examined latent interaction 
estimation mainly under nonnormal predictors 
[e.g., @Cham2012; @Vieira2026; @Lonati2025; @Brandt2014], 
and in only one case under a misspecified measurement model [@Brandt2020], 
but not within (moderated) mediation, despite its popularity in applied 
research. In fact, only a few studies investigated (moderated) mediation with 
latent variables, and those always assumed a correctly specified (and saturated) model 
[@FalkBiesanz2015; @Feng2020]. Two questions therefore remain open. 
The first concerns the robustness of the estimators to structural and measurement
misspecification, alone and in combination with nonnormal distributions, which is
relevant because the estimators rely on different distributional assumptions
[e.g., @Kelava2011; @Lonati2025; @Vieira2026].
Connected to this, the second concerns how misspecification in the measurement
model may affect the estimates, a question that is
particularly relevant in moderated mediation, where the mediator functions simultaneously
as an outcome and a predictor, and the effect of interest is a nonlinear (product)
function of two structural coefficients.

### Present Research and Contributions

This study evaluates LSAM, alongside LMS and a PI approach, for latent variable
moderated mediation. We assess the three estimators across a wide range of
conditions and on a broad set of performance measures, both detailed in the
next section. Thus, this study has three contributions to the literature.

These estimators have not been evaluated under misspecification of the
measurement or the structural model in a large setting. Moreover, the LSAM
approach has not been assessed for derived quantities such as the imm, 
which is a nonlinear functions of the structural parameters whose behavior 
need not match that of their constituents. Moreover, for such quantities inference has 
relied on the bootstrap [@Hayes2015; @Tibbe2022], especially in the 
limited prior work concerning (moderated) mediation with latent variables 
[@FalkBiesanz2015; @Feng2020]. In this study, we adopt and evaluate 
the Monte Carlo method, as it may be accurate enough without 
the computational cost of the bootstrap, which can itself underperform at 
small sample sizes.

```{r}
#| include: false
# smoke-test for the knitr engine. add library() calls and data reads
# (e.g. readRDS("../results/summary.rds")) — plus the matching packages in
# gen_env.R's r_pkgs — as the manuscript needs them.
invisible(1 + 1)
```

# Simulation Design and Performance Evaluation

In this section we describe the design of the simulation study and the
measures used to evaluate performance, following recent recommendations for 
the conduct and reporting of simulation studies [@Morris2019; @Siepe2024; 
@Pawel2026].

### Population Model and Estimand

We study a moderated mediation model among four latent variables: an
exogenous predictor $X$, a moderator $W$, a mediator $M$, and an outcome $Y$.
The structural model is as follows

$$
M = a_1 X + a_2 W + a_3\,(X \times W) + \zeta_M, \qquad
Y = b\,M + c'\,X + \zeta_Y,
$$

The primary estimands are the index of moderated mediation (imm) [@Hayes2015],
i.e., $a_3 b$. The remaining structural coefficients ($a_1$, $a_2$, $b$, $c'$) 
and the twelve freely estimated factor loadings serve as secondary estimands. 
Because the index is a derived quantity rather than a free parameter, its 
true value is computed as $a_3 b$ implied by the correctly specified model.

Population values were fixed at $a_1 = 0.4$, $a_2 = 0.3$, $b = 0.5$, and
$c' = 0.15$, with $\mathrm{cor}(X, W) = 0.3$. The interaction coefficient $a_3$
is a design factor (see below). These coefficients, together with the structural
disturbance variances, were chosen so that under the baseline condition (normal
exogenous predictors and a correctly specified model) each dependent latent
variable has $R^2 = 0.30$ at the interaction level $a_3 = 0.20$. Note that, 
because $a_3$ contributes to the variance of $M$, the explained variance is 
naturally higher at $a_3 = 0.40$ and lower at $a_3 = 0$. Each latent variable is 
measured by four congeneric indicators with loadings $1.0$, $0.8$, $0.7$, 
and $0.6$. @fig-model depicts the population
model, where the structural misspecifications studied below are drawn in grey.

```{tikz}
%| label: fig-model
%| fig-cap: "Population (Data-Generating) Model for the Simulation Study."
%| apa-note: "*Note*. $X$, $W$, $M$, and $Y$ are latent variables, each measured by four congeneric indicators; circles are latent variables and $X{\\times}W$ is the latent interaction. Black elements form the correctly specified model that every estimator fits ($a_1$, $a_2$, $a_3$, $b$, $c'$, and the $X$--$W$ covariance). Grey elements are the misspecifications present in the population but omitted from the fitted model: two in the structural part, an omitted moderator effect on the outcome ($c_2$) and an omitted moderated direct effect ($c_3$); and two in the measurement part, an omitted cross-loading of $X$ on $m_3$ (cl) and an omitted residual covariance between $m_3$ and $y_3$ (rc). Residual variances and intercepts are not shown."
%| fig-align: center
%| fig-width: 9
%| fig-height: 6

\tikzset{
  latent/.style  ={circle,draw,minimum size=12mm},
  ind/.style     ={rectangle,draw,minimum height=6mm,minimum width=7mm,align=center},
  derived/.style ={circle,draw,minimum size=12mm},
  mis/.style     ={->,gray!70,thick,dashed},
  miscov/.style  ={<->,gray!70,thick,dashed},
  every node/.style={font=\small}
}

\begin{tikzpicture}

% X
\node[ind] (x1) at (0,  5.4) {$x_1$};
\node[ind] (x2) at (0,  4.6) {$x_2$};
\node[ind] (x3) at (0,  3.8) {$x_3$};
\node[ind] (x4) at (0,  3.0) {$x_4$};
\node[latent] (X) at (2.6, 4.2) {$X$};
\draw[->] (X.west) -- (x1.east);
\draw[->] (X.west) -- (x2.east);
\draw[->] (X.west) -- (x3.east);
\draw[->] (X.west) -- (x4.east);

% moderator W
\node[ind] (w1) at (0,  1.4) {$w_1$};
\node[ind] (w2) at (0,  0.6) {$w_2$};
\node[ind] (w3) at (0, -0.2) {$w_3$};
\node[ind] (w4) at (0, -1.0) {$w_4$};
\node[latent] (W) at (2.6, 0.2) {$W$};
\draw[->] (W.west) -- (w1.east);
\draw[->] (W.west) -- (w2.east);
\draw[->] (W.west) -- (w3.east);
\draw[->] (W.west) -- (w4.east);

% interaction
\node[derived] (XW) at (2.6, -3.2) {$X{\times}W$};

% X-W covariance (part of the fitted model)
\draw[<->] (X) to[bend right=42] (W);

% m
\node[latent] (M) at (7.0, 2.2) {$M$};
\node[ind] (m1) at (5.5, 5.2) {$m_1$};
\node[ind] (m2) at (6.5, 5.2) {$m_2$};
\node[ind] (m3) at (7.5, 5.2) {$m_3$};
\node[ind] (m4) at (8.5, 5.2) {$m_4$};
\draw[->] (M.north) -- (m1.south);
\draw[->] (M.north) -- (m2.south);
\draw[->] (M.north) -- (m3.south);
\draw[->] (M.north) -- (m4.south);

% outcome y
\node[latent] (Y) at (11.4, 2.2) {$Y$};
\node[ind] (y1) at (13.8, 3.4) {$y_1$};
\node[ind] (y2) at (13.8, 2.6) {$y_2$};
\node[ind] (y3) at (13.8, 1.8) {$y_3$};
\node[ind] (y4) at (13.8, 1.0) {$y_4$};
\draw[->] (Y.east) -- (y1.west);
\draw[->] (Y.east) -- (y2.west);
\draw[->] (Y.east) -- (y3.west);
\draw[->] (Y.east) -- (y4.west);

% correct struct paths
\draw[->] (X)  -- node[above,sloped,font=\footnotesize] {$a_1$} (M);
\draw[->] (W)  -- node[below,sloped,font=\footnotesize] {$a_2$} (M);
\draw[->] (XW) -- node[below,sloped,font=\footnotesize] {$a_3$} (M);
\draw[->] (M)  -- node[above,font=\footnotesize] {$b$} (Y);
\draw[->] (X)  to[bend left=22] node[above,font=\footnotesize] {$c'$} (Y);

% misspecifications (grey): c2/c3 structural, cl/rc measurement
\draw[mis] (W)  to[bend right=24] node[below,sloped,font=\footnotesize,text=gray] {$c_2$} (Y);
\draw[mis] (XW) to[bend right=34] node[below,sloped,font=\footnotesize,text=gray] {$c_3$} (Y);
\draw[mis] (X) to[out=68,in=128] node[above,font=\footnotesize,text=gray,pos=0.62] {cl} (m3.north);
\draw[miscov] (m3.north) to[out=42,in=150] node[above,sloped,font=\footnotesize,text=gray,pos=0.5] {rc} (y3.north west);

\end{tikzpicture}
```

### Simulation Conditions

We employed a fully factorial design crossing five factors: sample size
($N \in \{100, 200, 300, 500\}$), interaction magnitude
($a_3 \in \{0, 0.20, 0.40\}$), measurement reliability (low $= 0.5$,
high $= 0.7$), the distribution of the exogenous latent predictors (normal, 
uniform, $t$, and two right-skewed $\chi^2$ distributions), 
and the (mis)specification condition (nine levels; see
below). This yielded $4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 5 \times 9 = 1{,}080$ 
conditions, each replicated 800 times.

Sample sizes span the small-to-moderate range typical of applied moderated
mediation research, where interaction terms are estimated with limited power
[@Hayes2015; @Preacher2007]. The interaction magnitudes include a null condition
($a_3 = 0$) to assess Type I error and false-positive coverage of the index, and
two nonzero levels representing small and moderate latent moderation effects.
Reliability levels of $0.5$ and $0.7$ were chosen because measurement error is
amplified in product terms and is therefore especially consequential for latent
interaction estimation [@Bollen1989; @Brandt2014], and because lower reliability
is common in applied settings than the values typically examined in prior work.
Nonnormality of the latent predictors is both empirically common and 
methodologically central for latent interaction estimators, whose distributional 
assumptions differ across approaches [@KleinMoosbrugger2000; @KleinMuthen2007; 
@Cham2012]. The misspecification conditions follow the moderated-mediation 
framework for the two structural omissions [@Preacher2007] and prior 
latent-interaction simulation work for the two measurement omissions 
[@Brandt2020]. They were chosen as interpretable threats, acting respectively 
as a measured mediator–outcome confounder, a conflated moderated direct effect, 
exposure-correlated measurement error, and an unmeasured residual confounder 
[@VanderWeele2015; @Edwards2007]. The nine levels are detailed below.

### Estimators

We compared a two-step estimator against two system-wide estimators. The
two-step approach is LSAM [@RosseelLoh2024; @Rosseel2025]. In this case, the 
interacting predictors $X$ and $W$ shared a single measurement block, 
while $M$ and $Y$ formed their own blocks. The two
system-wide estimators are LMS [@KleinMoosbrugger2000] and the extended
unconstrained product-indicator approach with double mean centering, which we
refer to as UPI throughout [@KelavaBrandt2009; @Marsh2004; @Lin2010].

### Data Generation

Appendix A describes the complete data-generating mechanism in detail. Briefly,
the latent variables $X$ and $W$ were drawn from a bivariate
distribution with $\operatorname{Cor}(X, W) = \rho = 0.3$, constructed with the
vine-to-anything (VITA) algorithm [@Gronneberg2022; @Foldnes2015]. Five exogenous
distributions were examined, each standardized to mean $0$ and variance $1$ so
that branches differ in shape rather than scale or location: normal, 
$X, W \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$,
using a forced Gaussian copula. The remaining four combine non-normal margins 
with a non-Gaussian vine copula:
uniform, $t_5$, and two $\chi^2(2)$ conditions with $X$ and $W$ skewed in the 
same or in opposite directions. Given that, $M$ and $Y$ were generated from the 
structural equations above with independent Gaussian disturbances 
$\zeta_M \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \psi_M)$ and $\zeta_Y \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \psi_Y)$, 
and each latent variable's four 
indicators were formed as $z_{\eta j} = \lambda_j \eta + \delta_{\eta j}$, 
with loadings $\lambda = (1, 0.8, 0.7, 0.6)$ and Gaussian errors
$\delta_{\eta j} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \theta_{\eta j})$ whose variances 
$\theta_{\eta j}$ were scaled to the target reliability $\omega \in \{0.5, 0.7\}$.

The nine (mis)specification conditions comprise one correctly specified condition
and four families of misspecification, each at two empirically calibrated 
magnitudes (standardized $0.25$ and $0.50$, detailed in Appendix A). 
Two are structural: an omitted direct effect of the moderator on the outcome 
(`c2`) and an omitted moderated direct effect (`c3`). The others are in the 
measurement model: a cross-loading of a mediator indicator on $X$ (`cl`) and a 
residual covariance between a mediator and an outcome indicator (`rc`). 
In every case the effect is present in the 
population but omitted from the fitted model.

### Nonconvergence, Outliers, and Inadmissible Solutions

Following previous research [@Lonati2025; @Vieira2026], replications were screened
for estimation failures and inadmissible solutions before analysis. Because the 
compared estimators are implemented across different packages, we applied an 
identical admissibility check to all of them: a solution was flagged when any 
residual (unique) variance was nonpositive or when the latent covariance matrix 
was not positive definite. Each estimator was fit independently within a 
replication, so a failure of one method produced a missing row for that method 
only, with the captured error or warning retained for diagnostics. 

### Performance Measures

Performance was assessed with the measures implemented in the *simhelpers*
package [@Joshi2025], following recommendations in @Morris2019. For the index of
moderated mediation and the structural coefficients we report relative bias and
relative RMSE, the mean standard error, the SE/SD ratio and the relative bias of
the variance estimator, confidence-interval coverage, and—where applicable—the
rejection rate (Type I error when $a_3 = 0$, power otherwise). Monte Carlo
uncertainty in each performance measure is quantified with Monte Carlo standard
errors [@Joshi2025], computed by jackknife where no closed-form expression is
available. @tbl-performance lists the empirical and Monte Carlo standard-error
calculations for each measure.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-performance
#| tbl-cap: "Subset of the Performance Measures for Evaluating Parameter Estimates"
#| apa-note: "$B_k$ is the estimate of parameter $\\theta$ in replication $k$ ($k = 1, \\dots, K$ usable replications), $\\bar{B}$ its mean, $S_B^2$ its sample variance, and $S_B$ its standard deviation; $\\mathrm{RMSE} = \\sqrt{\\tfrac{1}{K}\\sum_k (B_k-\\theta)^2}$; $\\widehat{SE}_k$ is the estimated standard error, $\\overline{SE^2} = \\tfrac{1}{K}\\sum_k \\widehat{SE}_k^2$, and $L_k, U_k$ the confidence-interval limits; $c$ and $r$ denote coverage and rejection rate, and $\\rho$ the relative bias of the variance estimator. The subscript $(j)$ marks a leave-one-out jackknife replicate. The MCSEs for relative RMSE and the relative bias of the variance estimator are computed by jackknife; the others are closed-form, and dashes mark MCSEs that are not computed. All measures were computed with the simhelpers package."
g <- function(x) paste0("\\textit{", x, "}")
tab <- data.frame(
  measure = c(
    g("Relative criteria"),
    "Relative bias", "Relative RMSE",
    g("Standard errors"),
    "Mean SE", "SE/SD ratio", "Relative bias of variance estimator",
    g("Coverage"),
    "Coverage",
    g("Hypothesis testing"),
    "Rejection rate"),
  empirical = c(
    "",
    "$\\tfrac{\\bar{B}}{\\theta}-1$",
    "$\\tfrac{1}{|\\theta|}\\sqrt{\\tfrac{1}{K}\\sum_{k=1}^{K}(B_k-\\theta)^2}$",
    "",
    "$\\overline{SE}=\\tfrac{1}{K}\\sum_{k=1}^{K}\\widehat{SE}_k$",
    "$\\overline{SE}/S_B$",
    "$\\rho=\\overline{SE^2}/S_B^2$",
    "",
    "$c=\\tfrac{1}{K}\\sum_{k=1}^{K}I(L_k\\le\\theta\\le U_k)$",
    "",
    "$r=\\tfrac{1}{K}\\sum_{k=1}^{K}I(0\\notin[L_k,U_k])$"),
  mcse = c(
    "",
    "$\\sqrt{S_B^2/(K\\theta^2)}$",
    "$\\tfrac{1}{|\\theta|}\\sqrt{\\tfrac{K-1}{K}\\sum_{j=1}^{K}(\\mathrm{RMSE}_{(j)}-\\mathrm{RMSE})^2}$",
    "",
    "---", "---",
    "$\\sqrt{\\tfrac{K-1}{K}\\sum_{j=1}^{K}(\\rho_{(j)}-\\rho)^2}$",
    "",
    "$\\sqrt{c(1-c)/K}$",
    "",
    "$\\sqrt{r(1-r)/K}$"))
knitr::asis_output(paste0("\\begin{center}\n",
  knitr::kable(tab, booktabs = TRUE, escape = FALSE, longtable = TRUE,
               linesep = "", format = "latex",
               col.names = c("Performance measure", "Empirical calculation", "MCSE")),
  "\n\\end{center}"))
```

Inference for the index of moderated mediation accounts for the skewness of the
product $a_3 b$: for $r = 1, \dots, R$ (with $R = 20{,}000$) we drew

$$
\bigl(a_3^{(r)}, b^{(r)}\bigr) \sim
\mathcal{N}_2\!\bigl((\hat a_3, \hat b),\, \hat\Sigma_{a_3 b}\bigr),
\qquad
\mathrm{imm}^{(r)} = a_3^{(r)}\, b^{(r)},
$$

where $\hat\Sigma_{a_3 b}$ is the estimated covariance of $(\hat a_3, \hat b)$,
and took the $2.5$th and $97.5$th empirical percentiles of
$\{\mathrm{imm}^{(r)}\}_{r=1}^{R}$ as the interval. The structural coefficients
and loadings use Wald (?) intervals from the model-implied parameter covariance
matrix. The same
implementation was applied to all three estimators for comparability.

### Reproducibility and Computational Details

All analyses were conducted in R [@RCoreTeam] using the *lavaan* [@Rosseel2012]
and *modsem* [@Slupphaug2025] packages for estimation, *covsim* [@Gronneberg2022]
and *rvinecopulib* [@Nagler2025] for data generation, and *simhelpers*
[@Joshi2025] for performance evaluation. Conditions were run in parallel with 
the *parallel* package. Reproducible and independent random-number streams 
across workers were obtained with the L'Ecuyer-CMRG generator. 
The computational environment was pinned with Nix via the *rix* package [@rix] 
to ensure reproducibility of the computational environment, and the article was 
written with the *apaquarto* extension [@apaquarto]. For the package versions, 
code and materials, see the project repository.

# Results

We present the results in three sections. We first report the imm and the
interaction coefficient. We then turn to the remaining structural
coefficients (i.e., $a_1$, $a_2$, $b$, and $c'$), and we close with the
estimated factor loadings, focusing on the cross-loaded indicator. 
Due to the large number of conditions, the figures show a focused subset 
of the results. We fix the interaction coefficient at 0.2 
(except for Type I error, which by construction requires a zero interaction) 
and display a limited number of sample sizes: $N = 200$ and
$500$ for the imm and the interaction coefficient, and $N = 500$ for the
structural coefficients and loadings. For the latter two we further restrict to
high reliability, whereas the imm and the interaction coefficient are shown at
both reliability levels. The entire set of results, across all sample sizes,
reliabilities, and interaction values, is available in the repository.

## Main Structural Estimates

### Relative Bias

Under correct specification with normal predictors, all three estimators recover
both the imm and $a_3$ with negligible bias (@fig-bias). In fact, bias is 
negligible across the nonnormal predictors as well, with the exception of the 
$\chi^2$ conditions, where LMS and LSAM are the most affected. In this case, 
however, larger sample size removes the bias for LSAM but not for LMS,
while higher reliability lessens it for both. Under misspecification, 
both `c2` and `c3` bias the imm for all three estimators, as expected. 
For $a_3$, `c2` produces no bias, whereas `c3` leads to bias under the system-wide 
estimators. Among the measurement omissions, `cl` attenuates $a_3$ only under the 
system-wide estimators, while for the imm LMS shows bias in the case of $\chi^2$ 
conditions. Last, the `rc` leaves $a_3$ and the imm essentially unchanged. 
In all cases the bias increases with the magnitude of the omission and at lower reliability.

Note the bias of $a_3$ under `c3` is the one result not implied by the omission 
alone. As both omissions sit in the outcome equation, the mediator equation 
that defines $a_3$ stays correct. Hence, LSAM, which estimates $a_3$ from the 
mediator block alone, recovers it. The system-wide estimators fit the full 
covariance structure, where the latent interaction reaches the outcome 
only through $a_3$ and $b$, and absorb the omitted covariance into $a_3$.

### Relative RMSE

Under correct specification, relative RMSE falls with sample size and reliability
for all three throughout distributions (@fig-rmse). Specifically, LMS is the most efficient, 
LSAM intermediate, and UPI the least. However, under misspecification, 
the structural omissions (`c2` and `c3`) inflate the RMSE of the imm for all three estimators. 
In fact, such increase, in some cases, affects the system-wide estimators the most. 
With respect to the measurement omissions (`cl` and `rc`), we observed a negligible 
effect. For $a_3$, only `c3` inflates slightly the RMSE mainly for UPI, while LSAM and 
LMS are unaffected. The inflation is larger at lower reliability.

### Coverage

Under correct specification with normal predictors, coverage is around the
nominal level with all three approaches (@fig-coverage-imm and @fig-coverage-a3) 
for the imm and $a_3$. It goes down slightly under heavy-tailed ($t_5$) and 
$\chi^2$ predictors for LMS and UPI, respectively, but mainly with a 
smaller sample size and low reliability.

Under misspecification, `c2` and `c3` reduce the imm coverage, since its interval
is then centered on a biased estimate, with the largest drop for the system-wide
estimators under `c3`. For $a_3$, the cross-loading (`cl`) is the damaging case,
collapsing coverage under the system-wide estimators at low reliability while LSAM
holds near nominal. The remaining omissions leave coverage close to nominal.

### Standard errors

Under correct specification the SE/SD ratio is near one for all three approaches
(@fig-sesd-imm and @fig-sesd-a3) in the case of a high reliability. Indeed, an 
overestimation is observed in case of low reliability and smaller sample size for UPI.
Moreover, in the $\chi^2$ in opposite directions, LSAM and UPI struggled the most 
throughout all misspecifications, including with the correct specification. 
These results were highly similar for both imm and $a_3$. We also observe that the measurement
misspecifications affect in particular LMS in combination with specific nonnormal distributions.
Similar conclusions are obtained with the relative bias of the variance estimator 
(@fig-relbiasvar-imm and @fig-relbiasvar-a3).

Under misspecification the SE/SD ratio remains near one for all three. The
variance-bias measure stays near one at high reliability but becomes unstable at
low reliability, where a few extreme replications inflate it for every method and
most severely for LMS, with the largest cells truncated in the figures.

### Hypothesis Testing (Type I error and Power)

Under correct specification with normal predictors and $a_3 = 0$, the imm test
holds its nominal level reasonably well (@fig-typei). UPI is slightly
conservative, LSAM is closest to nominal, and LMS is liberal, with the same
ordering for $a_3$. The inflation of LMS grows under nonnormal predictors,
exceeding the nominal level in several skewed and heavy-tailed cells, while LSAM
and UPI remain near it.

Under misspecification, `c3` inflates the false-positive rate of the system-wide
estimators, LMS the most, while LSAM holds its level. The cross-loading (`cl`)
likewise inflates LMS, mainly at low reliability. The remaining omissions leave
the rate near nominal.

At $a_3 = 0.2$ the index is detected with power that rises with sample size and
reliability (@fig-power). LMS has the highest power throughout and UPI the lowest,
with LSAM close to LMS, mirroring their efficiency ordering. Power falls under
opposite-skew $\chi^2$ predictors, dropping well below the conventional $0.80$ at
the smaller sample size, and most for UPI and LSAM. We note however that interpreting 
power in the case of bias and inflated Type I error should be avoided [@Morris2019].

## Additional Structural Estimates

For the remaining structural coefficients ($a_1$, $a_2$, $b$, and
$c'$), at high reliability and $N = 500$, we report their relative bias
(@fig-param-bias), coverage (@fig-param-coverage), and SE/SD ratio
(@fig-param-sesd) across all misspecification conditions and predictor
distributions.

The coefficients show where each omission is absorbed. The two structural
omissions act on the outcome equation: `c2` biases the mediator-outcome slope $b$
and, more strongly, the direct effect $c'$, and `c3` is absorbed mainly by $b$.
Under LSAM the mediator-equation coefficients $a_1$ and $a_2$ stay essentially
unbiased under both structural omissions, since LSAM estimates the mediator block
in isolation; the system-wide estimators, by contrast, leak `c2` into the mediator
equation, biasing $a_2$ (about 11% at the medium level) and, to a lesser extent,
$a_1$, and likewise carry part of `c3` into $c'$. The cross-loading (`cl`) is the
most disruptive, biasing all four coefficients for every estimator, because the
omitted $X$ loading on $m_3$ distorts the mediator's relations to the other latent
variables. The residual covariance (`rc`) slightly inflates $b$ and biases $c'$
downward for all three. Across conditions the bias is consistently smaller under
LSAM than under the system-wide estimators.

Taken together, under correct specification the three estimators recover the imm
and $a_3$ with little bias and near-nominal coverage, and differ mainly in a
calibration and efficiency trade-off. LMS is the most efficient but runs liberal
and is the most sensitive to nonnormality. LSAM is the best calibrated in both
coverage and Type I error at a modest efficiency cost. UPI is intermediate but
unstable at the smallest sample size.

```{r}
#| label: fig-bias
#| fig-cap: "Relative Bias of the Index of Moderated Mediation (imm) and the Interaction Coefficient $a_3$"
#| out-width: "6.3in"
knitr::include_graphics("../plots/combo_rel_bias_a3-0.2.png")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-rmse
#| fig-cap: "Relative RMSE of the Index of Moderated Mediation (imm) and the Interaction Coefficient $a_3$"
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("../plots/both_rel_rmse_a3-0.2.png")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-coverage-imm
#| fig-cap: "Coverage of the Index of Moderated Mediation"
#| out-width: "6.3in"
knitr::include_graphics("../plots/imm_coverage_a3-0.2_byrel.png")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-coverage-a3
#| fig-cap: "Coverage of the Interaction Coefficient $a_3$"
#| out-width: "6.3in"
knitr::include_graphics("../plots/a3_coverage_a3-0.2_byrel.png")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-sesd-imm
#| fig-cap: "SE/SD of the Index of Moderated Mediation"
#| out-width: "6.3in"
knitr::include_graphics("../plots/imm_se_sd_a3-0.2_byrel.png")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-sesd-a3
#| fig-cap: "SE/SD of the Interaction Coefficient $a_3$"
#| out-width: "6.3in"
knitr::include_graphics("../plots/a3_se_sd_a3-0.2_byrel.png")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-relbiasvar-imm
#| fig-cap: "Relative Bias of the Variance Estimator of the Index of Moderated Mediation"
#| out-width: "6.3in"
knitr::include_graphics("../plots/imm_rel_bias_var_a3-0.2_byrel.png")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-relbiasvar-a3
#| fig-cap: "Relative Bias of the Variance Estimator of the Interaction Coefficient $a_3$"
#| out-width: "6.3in"
knitr::include_graphics("../plots/a3_rel_bias_var_a3-0.2_byrel.png")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-typei
#| fig-cap: "Type I Error of the Index of Moderated Mediation (imm) and the Interaction Coefficient $a_3$"
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("../plots/both_typeI_a3-0.png")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-power
#| fig-cap: "Power for the Index of Moderated Mediation (imm) and the Interaction Coefficient $a_3$ at $a_3 = 0.2$"
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("../plots/both_power_a3-0.2.png")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-param-bias
#| fig-cap: "Relative Bias of the Structural Coefficients ($a_1$, $a_2$, $b$, $c'$) at High Reliability and $N = 500$"
#| out-width: "6.3in"
knitr::include_graphics("../plots/param_rel_bias_a3-0.2_n500_high.png")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-param-coverage
#| fig-cap: "Coverage of the Structural Coefficients ($a_1$, $a_2$, $b$, $c'$) at High Reliability and $N = 500$"
#| out-width: "6.3in"
knitr::include_graphics("../plots/param_coverage_a3-0.2_n500_high.png")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-param-sesd
#| fig-cap: "SE/SD of the Structural Coefficients ($a_1$, $a_2$, $b$, $c'$) at High Reliability and $N = 500$"
#| out-width: "6.3in"
knitr::include_graphics("../plots/param_se_sd_a3-0.2_n500_high.png")
```

## Measurement Loadings

Finally, for the twelve freely estimated factor loadings, all but one are
recovered with near-nominal performance across every measure and condition. 
Indeed, as expected, the cross-loaded mediator indicator $m_3$: under the
omitted cross-loading (`cl`) its relative bias rises and its coverage collapses,
while the other eleven loadings stay flat throughout. One subtler pattern is worth
noting, even though it is negligible in size: under the residual covariance
(`rc`), the system-wide estimators carry a systematic upward bias in $m_3$, well
under one percent, whereas LSAM stays centered on zero, since it estimates the
mediator's measurement block in isolation. We report only $m_3$, with its relative
bias, coverage, SE/SD ratio, and variance bias shown together (@fig-load) at high
reliability and $N = 500$.

```{r}
#| label: fig-load
#| fig-cap: "Performance of the Cross-Loaded Mediator Indicator Loading ($m_3$) Across All Measures at High Reliability and $N = 500$"
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics("../plots/load_lm3_allmeasures_a3-0.2_n500_high.png")
```

# Discussion

We evaluated three estimators of a latent variable moderated mediation model, the
two-step LSAM and the system-wide LMS and UPI, under correct specification and
under a large number of structural and measurement misspecifications. Two questions
organized the study: how the estimators recover the index of moderated mediation
and its constituents when the model is correct, and how each responds when the
fitted model omits an effect that is present in the population.

Under correct specification the three estimators agree on the substance. All
recover the imm and the interaction coefficient with negligible bias and
near-nominal coverage, and they separate mainly along a calibration and efficiency
trade-off. LMS is the most efficient, with the lowest RMSE throughout, but it runs
liberal and is the most sensitive to nonnormal predictors, where its Type I error
climbs above the nominal level. Note the bias is dependent on the type of nonnormality,
similar to previous findings [@Lonati2025; @Vieira2026]. LSAM is the best calibrated in
both coverage and Type I error, at a modest cost in efficiency. 
UPI sits between the two but does not yield usable estimates at the smallest sample size. 
For a correctly specified
model the choice is therefore between efficiency and calibration rather than
between right and wrong answers, and it depends on the sample size and on the
plausibility of normal predictors.

The estimators diverge under misspecification, and the divergence follows from
how each ties the equations together. Under system-wide estimation a
misspecification cannot be contained: because all parameters are fit jointly to
the full indicator covariance matrix, an omitted effect is reproduced through
whatever paths the model makes available and spreads into parameters that bear no
structural relation to it, including the interaction coefficient and the loadings,
biasing them and inflating the Type I error of the index. Structural omissions
mainly perturb the structural estimates, whereas measurement omissions matter most
in specific, demanding settings, in particular at low reliability—patterns broadly
consistent with previous work [@cite]. LSAM avoids this by estimating the
measurement model first and reading the structural parameters from blocks that do
not involve the misspecified equation; the separation does not make the structural
model correct, but it keeps a local error local. This is the interpretational
confounding that motivated the study, now visible in a moderated mediation setting
and in a derived quantity, the imm.

For practice, the results suggest a pragmatic division. When the measurement and
structural models are trusted and the predictors are approximately normal, LMS is
an efficient default, with the caveat that its standard errors run optimistic and
its Type I error is sensitive to nonnormality. When either assumption is in doubt,
and especially when the interaction coefficient or the index of moderated
mediation is the target, LSAM is the safer choice, because its estimate of the
interaction is insulated from misspecification elsewhere in the structural model
and from contamination in the measurement model. UPI is a reasonable middle option
at moderate and large sample sizes but should not be relied on when samples are
small. Across estimators, inference for the index by the Monte Carlo method was
well calibrated, which supports it as an alternative to the bootstrap for this
nonlinear quantity.

The study has limits that bound these conclusions [...]

Several extensions follow naturally [...]

{{< pagebreak >}}

# References

::: {#refs}
:::

{{< pagebreak >}}

# Data Generation and Calibration {#apx-calibration}

This appendix gives the formulas behind the data-generating mechanism. The full
derivations, with worked numerical examples, are in the project repository.

## Structural residual variances

The disturbance variances $\psi_M, \psi_Y$ are fixed so that each structural
equation explains a fraction $R^2 = 0.30$ of its outcome's variance at the
central interaction level $a_3 = 0.2$ under the normal baseline. Writing the
systematic part of $M$ as $S_M = a_1 X + a_2 W + a_3 (X W)$, its variance needs
only second moments,

$$
\operatorname{Var}(S_M) = a_1^2 + a_2^2 + 2 a_1 a_2 \rho + a_3^2 (1 + \rho^2),
$$

and the disturbance is the remainder that brings the signal share to $R^2$,

$$
\psi_M = \operatorname{Var}(S_M)\,\frac{1 - R^2}{R^2}, \qquad
\psi_Y = \operatorname{Var}(b M + c' X)\,\frac{1 - R^2}{R^2},
$$

with $\operatorname{Var}(b M + c' X) = b^2 \operatorname{Var}(M) + c'^2 + 2 b c'\,(a_1 + a_2 \rho)$.
Fixed at these values, the population variances of $M$ and $Y$ are closed-form
functions of the parameters,

$$
\operatorname{Var}(M) = \operatorname{Var}(S_M) + \psi_M, \qquad
\operatorname{Var}(Y) = b^2 \operatorname{Var}(M) + c'^2 + 2 b c'\,(a_1 + a_2 \rho) + \psi_Y,
$$

evaluated once under the normal baseline and held fixed across distributions, so
that $R^2$ and reliability drift only through the design factors.

## Indicator reliabilities

Each latent $\eta \in \{X, W, M, Y\}$ has four indicators
$z_{\eta j} = \lambda_j \eta + \delta_{\eta j}$ with $\lambda = (1, 0.8, 0.7, 0.6)$.
To hit a target reliability $\omega \in \{0.5, 0.7\}$, the residual variance is
set from the latent variance $V = \operatorname{Var}(\eta)$,

$$
\theta_{\eta j} = \lambda_j^2\, V\,\frac{1 - \omega}{\omega}.
$$

For $X, W$ the variance is exactly $1$; for $M, Y$ it is the closed form above,
fixed at the normal baseline, so the realized reliability drifts mildly across
distributions.

## Exogenous distributions

In every cell $(X, W)$ has mean $0$, variance $1$, and correlation $\rho = 0.3$;
only the shape changes. The uniform branch uses $\mathrm{U}(-\sqrt3, \sqrt3)$; the
$t_5$ branch samples with covariance $\tfrac{5}{3}\!\left[\begin{smallmatrix}1 & \rho \\ \rho & 1\end{smallmatrix}\right]$
and divides by $\sqrt{5/3}$; the $\chi^2_2$ branches center by $2$ and scale by
$\sqrt4$. `chisq_same` leaves both margins right-skewed, whereas `chisq_diff`
samples at $-\rho$ and negates $W$, flipping its skew while restoring
$\operatorname{Cor}(X, W) = +\rho$. Because $\operatorname{Var}(X W)$ is a
fourth-moment quantity, it is not constant across these conditions
($\approx 1.09$ under normality, larger under $t_5$ and $\chi^2$).

## Calibrating the misspecification magnitudes

The "small" and "medium" labels are standardized coefficients, $t \in \{0.25, 0.50\}$,
not raw ones. For an omitted predictor $p$ added to a baseline outcome $y_0$, the
magnitude is the fully standardized regression coefficient of $p$,

$$
t = \frac{\beta\,\operatorname{sd}(p)}{\operatorname{sd}(y_0 + \beta p)}.
$$

Squaring and substituting $\operatorname{Var}(y_0 + \beta p) = \operatorname{Var}(y_0) + \beta^2 \operatorname{Var}(p) + 2 \beta \operatorname{Cov}(p, y_0)$
yields a quadratic $A\beta^2 + B\beta + C = 0$ with

$$
A = \operatorname{Var}(p)\,(1 - t^2), \quad
B = -2 t^2 \operatorname{Cov}(p, y_0), \quad
C = -t^2 \operatorname{Var}(y_0).
$$

Since $A > 0$ and $C < 0$ the roots have opposite sign; the positive omitted path
is the positive root,

$$
\beta = \frac{t^2 \operatorname{Cov}(p, y_0) + \sqrt{\,t^4 \operatorname{Cov}(p, y_0)^2 + t^2 (1 - t^2)\,\operatorname{Var}(p)\,\operatorname{Var}(y_0)\,}}{\operatorname{Var}(p)\,(1 - t^2)}.
$$

The three moments are estimated from one large ($n = 10^6$) sample per
distribution and $a_3$ level. The predictor $p$ and baseline $y_0$ differ by
misspecification,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{c2}: \quad & p = W,   && y_0 = b M + c' X + \varepsilon_Y, \\
\texttt{c3}: \quad & p = X W, && y_0 = b M + c' X + \varepsilon_Y, \\
\texttt{cl}: \quad & p = X,   && y_0 = m_3 = \lambda_3 M + \delta_{m_3}.
\end{aligned}
$$

`c2` and `c3` are reliability-independent; `cl` depends on $\omega$ because its
baseline $m_3$ carries measurement noise. `rc` never enters the calibration: it
is a residual correlation and equals its target in every cell.

## Holding $Y$ reliability fixed under c2 and c3

Adding an omitted structural path inflates $\operatorname{Var}(Y)$ by
$\kappa = \operatorname{Var}(y_0 + \beta p) / \operatorname{Var}(y_0)$, which would
raise $Y$'s indicator reliability as a side effect. Scaling the $Y$-indicator
noise by the same $\kappa$ makes signal and noise move together, so $\kappa$
cancels and the reliability returns to its no-misspecification value,

$$
\omega_j = \frac{\kappa\,\lambda_j^2 V}{\kappa\,\lambda_j^2 V + \kappa\,\theta_j}
= \frac{\lambda_j^2 V}{\lambda_j^2 V + \theta_j}.
$$

The latent effect size is untouched; only the $Y$ indicators are made
correspondingly noisier. The measurement-level misspecifications `cl` and `rc`
act on the indicators directly and are not compensated, since that contamination
is itself the misspecification.

##
